Prep Instructions [012]:
1) Open all kale needed for the week and transfer into a blue strainer bin.
2) Place the bin in the fridge and allow the kale to air out throughout the day.
3) Weigh, label, and place back in the fridge.

1) Fill a clean sink with the hottest water possible (let the tap run for 1–2 minutes before plugging the drain).
2) Place frozen corn in a blue strainer bin and submerge it in the sink, allowing the hot water to fully cover the corn.
3) Quickly blanch the corn by letting it sit in the hot water for 3–5 minutes.
4) Drain the water from both the sink and the blue bin, then refill the sink with cold water to bring the corn to room temperature.
5) Once cool, drain the blue bin thoroughly and set it on a flipped, upside-down grey lid to catch excess water.
6) Weigh, label, and lid, then place in the fridge.

1) Wash all jalapeños thoroughly.
2) Tip and tail each jalapeño (remove stem and base).
3) Slice open lengthwise and remove seeds.
4) Set up the Juli Veg Punch Slicer with the small punch dicer.
5) Dice the jalapeños using the slicer.
6) Transfer diced jalapeños into a container.
7) Weigh, label, and store in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Confirm with the oven station leader whether feta will be made that day before starting tofu prep.
2) Remove tofu from packaging and gently drain off excess liquid. Slice each block into quarters using a clean knife—do not rip or tear the tofu.
3) Use the 1/2 inch hand punch dicer to dice the tofu quarters into even cubes, working carefully to avoid crumbling.
4) Place the diced tofu into clean grey bins. Weigh the product, label with the item name and date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Mise the pre-washed parsley.
2) Pluck leaves from any remaining thick stems, discarding only the large or woody ones—thin tender stems can be kept.
3) Gather the plucked parsley into a tight pile on the cutting board.
4) Using a sharp chef’s knife, chop the parsley with a smooth rocking motion, keeping the tip of the knife in contact with the board.
5) Re-gather the pile with your knife and rotate the board or pile to ensure an even chop.
6) Continue chopping until the parsley is finely chopped—smaller than a rough chop, but not minced.
7) Avoid over-chopping to prevent bruising and excess moisture.
8) Transfer the chopped parsley into a gray bin.
9) Weigh, label, and store in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Remove the tip and tail of each cucumber. Leave the skin on and keep the cucumbers whole — do not cut unless they are too large to fit into the hopper.
2) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E before setting it up. Open the continuous feed head and begin by placing the ejection disc onto the motor shaft inside the bowl. Next, insert the white “S” scraper blade, which helps move shredded product efficiently out of the machine. Then, mount the RG3 shredding blade (the smallest grater attachment) onto the shaft above the scraper. Make sure everything is seated securely. Close the lid and ensure it locks in place before use.
3) Plug in and turn on the Robot Coupe. Load cucumbers into the large feed chute and guide them through using the pusher, applying even pressure to produce a fine, consistent shred.
4) Once shredded, transfer the cucumbers to a fine-mesh strainer. Squeeze out as much liquid as possible and discard the juice. Place the drained cucumber shreds into a clean container, weigh, label and place in the fridge

1) Gather and inspect all eggplant that has been ordered. Set aside any damaged pieces.
2) Tip and tail each eggplant by removing the stem and bottom end.
3) Using a sharp chef’s knife, slice each eggplant in half lengthwise to create even halves.
4) Place the halved eggplant into a clean grey bin, arranging them neatly to avoid bruising.
5) Weigh, label with item name and date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Peel the carrots completely, then trim off the tip and tail. If the carrots are large or very long, cut them into roughly 3-inch pieces to help them fit into the hopper more easily and ensure even cuts.
2) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E before starting setup. Open the continuous feed head and begin by placing the ejection disc onto the motor shaft inside the bowl. Next, install the white “S” scraper blade if required by the julienne disc. Then, mount the 3x3mm julienne disc onto the shaft above the scraper or directly above the ejection disc, depending on the blade design. Make sure the disc is locked securely in place, then close the lid and ensure it is properly latched.
3) Plug in and turn on the Robot Coupe. Load carrots into the large feed chute and guide them through using the pusher, applying steady, even pressure for uniform matchstick cuts.
4) Once all carrots are julienned, collect them in a clean grey bin. Weigh, label with the item name and date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Plucked and fine chop basil much smaller than the rough chop.
2) Weigh and label.
3) Place into a Tupperware container.

1) Remove dill from the stem: Hold the dill bunch by the stems, and gently pull the leaves off. Discard the thicker, tougher stems. The tender, feathery leaves are the part youll be using.
2) Gather the dill leaves together: Lay the dill leaves flat on the cutting board, making sure they are arranged evenly to ensure an even cut.
3) Roll the dill leaves (optional): For a finer mince, you can roll the dill leaves into a tight bundle. This makes it easier to chop evenly.
4) Mince the dill: Using a sharp chefs knife, make small, careful cuts across the dill leaves, going back and forth in a rocking motion. Continue mincing until the dill is very small and finely chopped.
5) Once minced, use it immediately for your recipe or store it in a container.

1) Wash the ginger thoroughly to remove any dirt or debris. Do not peel the ginger — keep the skin intact.
2) Chop the ginger into 1-inch pieces, ensuring they are uniform for even processing.
3) Place the chopped ginger into a food processor. Pulse until the ginger is finely minced, making sure there are no large chunks remaining.
4) Check the freezer for any pre-frozen ginger. If available, pull it out and set aside.
5) For the freshly minced ginger, divide the product into 2 kg portions and store each portion in separate freezer bags. Seal the bags tightly.
6) Label each freezer bag with the item name, weight, and date, then store in the freezer.

1) Pluck fresh basil leaves off stem, ensuring any wilting/black leaves are getting composted.Toss any stems as well.
2) Place plucked basil leaves into a container.
3) Weigh, Label, and place in the assigned area in the fridge.

1) Gather your cilantro.
2) Keep the tender stems: You do not need to pick off individual leaves. The thin stems near the top are flavorful and soft — keep everything from the top half of the bunch.
3) Bunch the cilantro together on your cutting board, holding it tightly like a bouquet to make cutting easier.
4) Use a sharp chef’s knife, evenly cuts across the bunch. Chop using a rocking motion, working from one end of the bunch to the other.
5) Make 2–3 passes across the pile of cilantro if needed. You’re aiming for pieces roughly ½ inch in size — no need to be precise.
6) Do not over-chop: You want a loose, leafy texture. Over-chopping can bruise the cilantro and make it mushy or wet.
7) Once cilantro is chopped place in a container, weigh,label,and place in correct arrea in the fridge.

1) Pluck the parsley leaves from the stems, discarding only the thick, woody parts. Some tender stems can remain.
2) Using a sharp knife, roughly chop the parsley into pieces about 1 cm in size. This should be a loose, rustic cut — not minced. The pieces should be large enough to see and pick up individually, as this parsley will be used as a fresh garnish on dishes.
3) Use the small scale to weigh the chopped parsley in portions as needed.
4) Transfer into a clean Tupperware container.
5) Label with item name, weight, and date, then place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Peel the onions fully, removing any outer skin and blemishes. Once peeled, cut all onions into quarters through the root end.
2) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E and open the continuous feed head. Begin by placing the ejection disc onto the motor shaft. Then insert the 3mm slicing blade and close the lid securely.
3) Plug in the machine and turn it on. Load the halved onions into the hopper, packing it as full as possible. Slice all onions using steady, even pressure.
4) Receive the sliced onions into clean grey bins. Weigh, label with item name and date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Cut off the bottom of the stalks.
2) Wash and remove leaves.
3) Set up robo coupe with 5/16 blade. If smaller amount use punch dicer.
4) Cut in half and place in the hopper.
5) Process all celery and receive into grey bins.
6) Weigh and label.

1) Remove tofu from bags, then cut into 1/4. Please cut the tofu do not rip.
2) Dice tofu with the 8mm dicer attachment.
3) Place into grey bins, weigh and label.

1) Peel the onions completely. Cut them in half through the root, then trim off the tip (non-root end) from each half. If onions are very large, cut them into quarters to help them pass smoothly through the dicing grid.
2) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E before setup. Open the continuous feed head and begin by placing the ejection disc onto the motor shaft. Insert the 8x8mm (5/16in) dicing grid into the bowl, ensuring it is seated flat and secure. Install the matching knife blade on the shaft above the grid. Close and lock the lid in place.
3) Plug in and turn on the Robot Coupe. Feed onion halves (or quarters, if large) through the hopper, using the pusher to apply steady pressure.
4) Transfer all diced onions into a clean grey bin. Weigh, label with the item name and date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area. For small batches, use the manual punch dicer and cut onions into quarters before dicing.

1) Peel the red onions completely, then cut each one in half lengthwise. Trim off both the tip and root end.
2) For small batches, use the manual punch dicer with the smallest dice attachment. Cut the onion halves into quarters before punching. For larger amounts, set up the Robot Coupe CL52E by first placing the ejection disc on the motor shaft. Then insert the 8x8mm (5/16) dicing grid into the bowl and mount the matching knife blade on the shaft above the grid. Close and lock the lid securely.
3) Feed red onion halves (or quarters, if large) through the hopper, applying steady pressure with the pusher to achieve a uniform dice.
4) Transfer diced onions into a clean grey bin. Weigh, label with the item name and date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Wash the tomatoes thoroughly.
2) Slice each tomato in half to create a flat surface.
3) Using the punch press fitted with the small dice blade, punch all tomato halves.
4) Place the diced tomatoes into a gray bin.
5) Weigh, label, and store in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Peel, cut, and remove core the onions.
2) Install the ejection disc into the large Robot Coupe bowl.
3) Insert the 5/16 slicing disc into the bowl, then carefully place the dicing blade on top of the grid.
4) Lock the bowl securely onto the base. 5 )Feed the prepared onions through the feed chute using the pusher, processing until all onions are sliced.
5) Transfer the diced onions into a grey bin.
6) Weigh and label.

1) Peel and trim the red onions, cutting off both the root and stem ends. Slice the onion in half through the root to create two manageable halves.
2) Set up the Robot Coupe by installing the ejection disc onto the motor shaft, followed by the 1/1 inch dicer grid. Install the 1/1 inch dicer blade above the grid and close the lid securely.
3) Process the halved red onions by placing them into the hopper. Use steady, consistent pressure to feed the onions through the dicer, ensuring they are evenly diced.
4) Lay the diced red onions out on a parchment-lined baking tray in a thin, even layer to allow them to dry properly.
5) Place the tray in the fridge for 2 hours to allow the diced red onions to dry.
6) Weigh and label the diced red onions with the item name and date, then store them in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Once onions are peeled and trimmed, place into the Robo-Coupe with the 4/5 Inch blades.
2) Once diced, layout on a parchment-lined baking tray to dry in the fridge for 2 hours.

1) Peel onions + tip and tail.
2) Place in the large grey robot -coupe with the 4/5 inch dicing blade.
3) Dice all onions and weight +label

1) Rinse the whole broccoli crown under cold running water to remove any dirt or debris, then shake off excess water.
2) Place the broccoli on a cutting board and slice off the thick stem flush with the base of the florets. Discard the woody end.
3) Turn the crown upside down and cut through the main stalks that connect each large branch of florets.
4) Slice each large branch into smaller bite-sized florets, cutting through the stems where they naturally divide. Dice up the stems and use as well, discarding any woody stems.
5) Place in a bin and weigh and label

1) Wash each green pepper thoroughly under cold running water.
2) Tip and tail the pepper by cutting off the stem end and the very bottom (base).
3) Stand the pepper on one of its cut ends. Using a small paring knife, make a vertical incision from top to bottom to open the pepper.
4) Gently pull the two halves apart.
5) Use the tip of your knife or your fingers to lift out and discard the white core and any attached seeds.
6) Rinse the pepper halves briefly to remove any loose seeds, then pat dry with a clean towel or paper towel.
7) Place the deseeded halves into a grey bin.
8) Weigh the peppers, affix the appropriate label, and store in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Tip and tail each red pepper by cutting off the top (stem end) and bottom.
2) Stand the pepper upright, and make a vertical cut down one side. Carefully open the pepper flat like a sheet.
3) Trim out the white ribs and remove all seeds using the knife or your hands. Check both sides to ensure all seeds and membranes are removed.
4) Once cleaned, place the de-seeded peppers into a grey bin for processing.
5) Weigh, label with item name and date, and store in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Wash the yellow peppers thoroughly in cold water.
2) Tip and tail the peppers by slicing off the top (stem end) and bottom.
3) Stand the pepper upright and make a vertical cut down the side.
4) Open the pepper flat and run your knife along the inside to remove the white ribs and seeds.
5) Discard all seeds and membrane.
6) Place cleaned pepper pieces into a gray bin to be processed.
7) Weigh, label, and store in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Drain the pickles from their pickling liquid using a colander or strainer. Reserve some of the liquid in an empty jar in case you end up with more diced pickles than needed and want to store them for future use.
2) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E before beginning setup. Open the continuous feed head and begin by placing the ejection disc onto the motor shaft inside the bowl — this disc spins to push product out after cutting. Next, insert the 8mm dicing grid, making sure it is seated flat and aligned correctly. Then mount the matching 8mm slicing disc onto the shaft above the grid. This disc slices the pickles before they pass through the grid to form small, even cubes. Close the lid and make sure it locks securely — the machine will not run unless it is fully latched.
3) Plug in and turn on the Robot Coupe. Load pickles into the large feed chute and use the pusher to guide them through the slicing disc and dicing grid. Apply firm, steady pressure without forcing the product.
4) Once all pickles are diced, collect them in the designated grey bin. Label the container with the item name and current date, and store it in the assigned fridge location according to kitchen standards.

1) Remove any damaged outer leaves. Cut large heads in half or quarters. Remove the tough stem/core from each section by cutting it out at an angle.
2) Soak the cabbage pieces in cold water to loosen dirt or insects. Rinse thoroughly and spin dry using the salad spinner until moisture is removed.
3) If prepping a small amount, hand cut napa cabbage into 1-inch dice using a chef’s knife. For larger batches, set up the Robot Coupe: first insert the ejection disc, then place the 1x1 inch dicing grid, and finally secure the matching 1x1 inch dicing blade.
4) Feed cabbage pieces into the hopper and process. Place diced cabbage into sheet trays and dry overnight in fridge.
5) Weigh, label with item name and date, and store in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E and open the continuous feed head. Place the ejection disc onto the motor shaft, then insert the 1/1 inch (20mm) dicing grid into the bowl and attach the matching knife blade on the shaft above the grid. Close and lock the lid securely.
2) Plug in the machine and turn it on. Feed the prepared red peppers into the hopper, processing them with steady, even pressure until all are diced into the grey bin.
3) Weigh the diced peppers and record the weight. Label the parchment-lined trays with the item name, weight, and date, then spread the peppers in a single layer and place the trays in the fridge to dry for 2 hours.

1) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E and open the continuous feed head. Install the ejection disc onto the motor shaft first, then insert the 20mm (4/5 inch) dicing grid into the bowl and mount the matching dicing knife blade on the shaft above the grid. Close and lock the lid securely.
2) Plug in the machine and turn it on. Feed the prepared peppers into the hopper and process using steady, even pressure until all are diced into a clean grey bin.
3) Weigh the diced peppers and label parchment-lined trays with the product name, date, and weight. Spread the peppers out evenly in a single layer and place the trays in the fridge to dry for 2 hours.

1) Set up Robo Coupe with the 8x8mm (5/16in) dicing grid.
2) Place a grey bin underneath the Robo Coupe so they fall into the grey bin.
3) Weigh, label with the item name and date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Batch all ingredients together, ensuring even distribution.
2) Weigh, label, and placed in the assigned area.

1) Peel carrots and cut them lengthwise.
2) Install the ejection disc into the large Robot Coupe bowl.
3) Insert the 8mm slicing disc into the bowl.
4) Lock the bowl securely onto the base. 5 )Feed the carrots through the feed chute using the pusher, processing until all the carrots are cut into 1/4 inch half moon slices.
5) Transfer the cut carrots into a grey bin.
6) Weigh each container. Lid, label with the name, date, weight, and your initials.

1) Wash the zucchinis under cold running water.
2) Trim off both ends and slice each zucchini in half lengthwise.
3) Transfer to bins and weigh.
4) Arrange the zucchini cut-side up on sheet trays.
5) Place the trays in a tented rack and roll into the cooler to air-dry overnight.
6) Label the rack and roll with product name and date.

1) Set up a green cutting board and get a sharp knife.
2) Take 6-8 pieces of julienned peppers and cut across the length to create 1/4 inch diced pieces (ask your station lead or a sous for a demo if needed).
3) Repeat the process until you have finished. Place the product onto sheet trays with parchment to allow them to dry slightly before portioning.
4) Weigh your product. Label with the name, date, your initial and the weight.
5) Enter your weight into the Prep Master List.

1) Set up a green cutting board and get a sharp knife.
2) Take 6-8 pieces of julienned peppers and cut across the length to create 1/4 inch diced pieces (ask your station lead or a sous for a demo if needed).
3) Repeat the process until you have finished. Place the product onto sheet trays with parchment to allow them to dry slightly before portioning.
4) Weigh your product. Label with the name, date, your initial and the weight.
5) Enter your weight into the Prep Master List.

Ingredient Name [008]: Punch Sliced Julienne Yellow Peppers (Veg T)

Prep Instructions [012]:
1) De-stem celery and wash.
2) Using a knife, remove the tops and leaves. Cut into 2-3 pieces.
3) Install the ejection disc into the large Robot Coupe CL52.
4) Insert the 1/1" slicing blade into the bowl.
5) Lock the bowl securely onto the base.
6) Feed the celery pieces through the feed chute using the pusher, processing until all the stalks are cut into 1 inch slices.
7) Transfer the cut celery into a grey bin.
8) Weigh each container. Lid, label with the name, date, weight, and your initials.

1) Wash all potatoes thoroughly to remove dirt and debris.
2) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E before setup. Open the continuous feed head and begin by placing the ejection disc onto the motor shaft. Insert the 1/1 inch dicing grid into the bowl, and attach the matching knife blade on the shaft above the grid. Close the lid securely.
3) Plug in and turn on the Robot Coupe. Process all the potatoes through the hopper using steady, even pressure. The diced potatoes will fall directly into the grey bin.
4) Weigh the diced potatoes and record the weight without water for your master list. Label with the item name and date.
5) To prevent browning, fill the bin with water and place it in the fridge if we arent using right away.

1) Wash all yams thoroughly under cold running water to remove any dirt or surface debris. Do not peel the yams unless otherwise specified. Trim off any tough ends or blemishes.
2) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E before setup. Open the continuous feed head and begin by placing the ejection disc onto the motor shaft. Insert the 20mm (4/5) dicing grid into the bowl, then attach the matching dicing blade on the shaft above the grid. Close and lock the lid securely.
3) Plug in and turn on the Robot Coupe. Process all of the prepared yam pieces through the hopper using steady, even pressure. The diced yams will fall directly into a clean grey bin.
4) Weigh the diced yams and record the weight (without water) for your master list. Label the bin with the item name and date.

1) Wash all potatoes thoroughly to remove dirt and debris.
2) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E before setup. Open the continuous feed head and begin by placing the ejection disc onto the motor shaft. Insert the 20mm (4/5) dicing grid into the bowl, and attach the matching knife blade on the shaft above the grid. Close the lid securely.
3) Plug in and turn on the Robot Coupe. Process all the potatoes through the hopper using steady, even pressure. The diced potatoes will fall directly into the grey bin.
4) Weigh the diced potatoes and record the weight without water for your master list. Label with the item name and date.
5) To prevent browning, fill the bin with water and place it in the fridge if we arent using right away.

1) Plucked basil from the stems.
2) Fine chop basil much smaller than the rough chop.
3) Weigh, label, and place in the assigned area in the fridge.

Sub-Recipe Name [006]: Pico De Galo Salsa No Liquid
Prep Instructions [012]:
1) Mise all ingredients.
2) Batch all ingredients together. Mix well.
3) Weigh and label

Ingredient Name [008]: Small Punch Dice Red Onion (Veg W)

Prep Instructions [012]:
1) Place frozen edamame beans into a blue strainer bin, and pour hot tap water over them until fully thawed.
2) Once thawed, immediately run cold water over the beans to cool them, then allow them to drain completely.
3) Place a grey lid upside down underneath the strainer bin to catch any remaining water.
4) Weigh, label with item name and date, and store in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Ensure spinach is fresh and washed before starting.
2) Gather a manageable handful of spinach leaves and place them on a cutting board.
3) Using a sharp knife, rough chop the spinach into 1–2 inch pieces. This should be a quick, even chop—not too fine.
4) Transfer chopped spinach into a clean grey bin.
5) Weigh, label, and store in the fridge in the assigned area.

Sub-Recipe Name [006]: Punch Diced Red Pepper HOT DISH
Prep Instructions [012]:
1) Wash the bell peppers thoroughly. Remove the stems, slice in half, and fully de-seed. Remove any white inner ribs if needed.
2) If the peppers are being used in a cold dish, dice them by hand for a cleaner appearance and better texture. For all other uses, proceed with the Robot Coupe CL52E setup: unplug the machine, place the ejection disc on the motor shaft, insert the 20mm (4/5) dicing grid, then attach the matching knife blade on the shaft above it. Close and lock the lid.
3) Plug in the machine and process all peppers through the hopper with steady pressure. Transfer diced peppers into a clean grey bin.
4) Spread the diced peppers in a single layer on a parchment-lined baking tray and place in the fridge to air dry for 2 hours. This helps remove excess moisture.
5) After drying, weigh, label with the item name and date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Place the punch slicer into a grey bin.
2) Place 2-4 quarters of deseeded pepper onto the slicer with the length in the same direction of the blades. Press down to get long julienned pieces. Repeat the process.
3) Repeat the process. When finished, record your finished weight and place the peppers in a single layer on trays of parchment to dry.
4) Weigh it and label the bin(s) with the name, date, weight and your initials.
5) Enter the weight into the Prep Master List.

1) Slice the prepared peppers using the hand-punch julienne slicer. Use firm, even pressure to achieve consistent cuts.
2) Weigh the julienned peppers and record the total. Label parchment-lined trays with the product name, date, and weight.
3) Spread the peppers in a single, even layer on the trays and place them in the fridge to dry for 2 hours.

1) Open each package and place into a clean grey bin. Inspect them as you go looking for brown or wrinkled pea pods. Discard any you find. Repeat until you have the desired weight.
2) Place the punch slicer into a clean empty grey bin.
3) Place a handful of pea pods on to the punch slicer, adjust the pieces so they are all laying across the blades so the slicer cuts across the width of each pod. This will create small 1/2 in by 1/4 in pieces.
4) Using the handle, press down to slice the pods. Repeat the process until you have finished. Be sure to change out the grey bin if it is too full, and combine into one bin after slicing.
5) Weigh, label with the item name and date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Wash tomatoes.
2) Remove the core from the tomatoes.
3) Cut into slices and put through the punch dicer.
4) Process all tomatoes and place them into grey bins.
5) Weigh, label, and place into the fridge.

1) Rough chop all artichoke hearts using a sharp knife. Aim for each piece to be about the size of half an original heart — roughly uniform but still rustic.
2) Weigh, label, and store in the fridge in the assigned area.

**We can use the steams for this recipe*** ask Chef for a demo
1) Pluck the parsley leaves from the stems, discarding only the thick, woody parts. Some tender stems can remain.
2) Using a sharp knife, roughly chop the parsley into pieces about 1 cm in size. This should be a loose, rustic cut — not minced. The pieces should be large enough to see and pick up individually, as this parsley will be used as a fresh garnish on dishes.
3) Use the small scale to weigh the chopped parsley in portions as needed.
4) Transfer into a clean Tupperware container.
5) Label with item name, weight, and date, then place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Rinse green onions thoroughly and remove any wilted or damaged outer layers.
2) Trim off the root ends and about 1 inch from the top of the green part if dry or tough.
3) Cut green onions crosswise into 1/4-inch pieces using a sharp knife or slicer, as needed for the recipe.
4) Weigh the final product.
5) Label and store in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Remove any damaged outer leaves. De-stem the green cabbage, then cut it into quarters through the core to fit easily into the hopper.
2) Set up the small Robot Coupe by unplugging the machine. Place the ejection disc onto the motor shaft, then install the 1/8 (3mm) slicing blade. Lock the lid securely.
3) Plug in the machine and process all green cabbage quarters through the hopper using steady, even pressure. The cabbage should come out thinly shaved.
4) Transfer the shaved green cabbage into a clean grey bin. Weigh, label with the item name and date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Remove any damaged outer leaves from the cabbage, then cut the head into quarters and remove the core from each section. If the quarters are still too large to fit in the hopper, cut them into smaller wedges.
2) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E before setting up the machine. Open the continuous feed head and start by placing the ejection disc onto the motor shaft inside the bowl. Then, mount the 1/8 (3mm) slicing disc directly on top of the shaft, making sure it is seated securely. No scraper blade is needed for slicing discs. Close the lid and lock it in place.
3) Plug in and turn on the Robot Coupe. Feed the cabbage wedges into the large hopper and use the pusher to guide them through the slicing disc, applying steady pressure to produce thin, even shreds.
4) Once all cabbage is shaved, receive the sliced cabbage into a clean grey bin. Weigh, label with the item name and current date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) If prepping a small batch, slice the Napa cabbage by hand into thin julienne strips. For larger amounts, use the julienne punch slicer, cutting cabbage to fit as needed.
2) Place all julienned cabbage into a cold water bath and wash thoroughly to remove any dirt or debris.
3) After washing, transfer the cabbage into the salad spinner in batches and spin dry until moisture is removed.
4) Weigh the spun-dry cabbage, then transfer it into thin, even layers on parchment-lined trays. Label each tray clearly with the item name, date, and weight.
5) Place labeled trays on a covered rolling rack and store in the fridge to air dry overnight.

1) Check for any prepped yellow onions from previous batches and use older stock first if still in good condition.
2) Peel the yellow onions fully, removing any outer skin and blemishes. Once peeled, cut all onions in half through the root end.
3) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E and set it up by installing the ejection disc on the motor shaft, followed by the 3mm slicing blade. Close and lock the lid securely.
4) Plug in the machine and process the halved onions by loading them into the hopper, packing it as full as possible. Slice all onions using steady, consistent pressure.
5) Receive sliced onions into clean grey bins. Weigh, label with item name and date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Set up a green cutting board.
2) Using a knife, De-seed each pepper, removing the white pith, seeds and stem.
3) Cut each pepper into quarters.
4) Place the punch dicer in a clean grey bin.
5) Lift the punch handle and place a piece of pepper skin side down on the grid below it. Make sure nothing but the pepper is on the grid and press the punch down to dice the pepper into 1/4 pieces.
6) Weigh, label with the item name and date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area.

7) Weigh and label with the name, weight, date and your initials.

12) Transfer cooled grains into shallow gray bins. Weigh, label, lid, and store in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Fill 60L pot 2/3 full with hot tap water. Add in each pot 100g of salt and 70g of oil to the pot (solid squirt from the oil bottle)

4) Add the pasta to the water once boiling (max 10kg per pot), and stir very frequently to prevent from sticking.
5) Boil pasta until 80% cooked, around 9 minutes to cook.
6) While the pasta is cooking, prepare two orange pails of cold tap water to later pour over noodles to flash cool them.
7) Make sure to clean and reserve the sinks for the next steps. In this next step, you will have to work fast to ensure the pasta doesnt over cook.
8) Once the pasta is ready to be drained, place the pot on the cart with the sheet tray underneath, ask a kitchen friend to help you lift to the sink, Slowly pour out the boiling water from the pot.
9) Once majority of the boiling water has been drained from the pot, pour the pasta into the strainer bin. And pour the cold water ontop to stop the cooking process.
10) Making sure to stir the pasta to eliminate the hot spots. Check the pasta to make sure it is properly cool, if not add more hot water. Once cool make sure all pasta water has drained out.
11) Once cool place the blue strainer, on top of a gray bin lid to catch any access water. Lightly drizzle oil on the pasta and to prevent the pasta from sticking. Make sure to stir the pasta with the oil to ensure all pasta is coated.
12) Weigh, label and place in the assigned area in the fridge.

1) Place a large pot of water over the rocket burner and bring to a boil.

1) Fill 60L pot 2/3 full with hot tap water. Add in each pot 100g of salt and 70g of oil to the pot (solid squirt from the oil bottle)
2) Place the pot on the rocket burner on max heat and bring to a boil. Cover with a lid to speed the process up.
3) Weigh out the dry pasta into a home depot bucket max weigh is 10kgs.
4) Add the pasta to the water once boiling (max 10kg per pot), and stir very frequently to prevent from sticking.
5) Boil pasta until 80% cooked, around 10 minutes to cook.
6) While the pasta is cooking, prepare two orange pails of cold tap water to later pour over noodles to flash cool them.
7) Make sure to clean and reserve the sinks for the next steps. In this next step, you will have to work fast to ensure the pasta doesnt over cook.
8) Once the pasta is ready to be drained, place the pot on the cart with the sheet tray underneath, ask a kitchen friend to help you lift to the sink, Slowly pour out the boiling water from the pot.
9) Once majority of the boiling water has been drained from the pot, pour the pasta into the strainer bin. And pour the cold water ontop to stop the cooking process.
10) Making sure to stir the pasta to eliminate the hot spots. Check the pasta to make sure it is properly cool, if not add more hot water. Once cool make sure all pasta water has drained out.
11) Once cool place the blue strainer, on top of a gray bin lid to catch any access water. Lightly drizzle oil on the pasta and to prevent the pasta from sticking. Make sure to stir the pasta with the oil to ensure all pasta is coated.
12) Weigh, label and place in the assigned area in the fridge.

1) Fill a 60 L pot with hot tap water. Add about 70 g of canola oil.
2) Place the strainer attachment into the pot.
3) Cover and bring to a boil on a rocket burner.
4) Weigh udon into bins — maximum 5 kg per pot.
5) Fill a large grey bin halfway with cold water and ice for cooling noodles later.
6) Add 5 kg of udon to the boiling water.
7) Stir very frequently to break up noodles and prevent sticking.
8) Boil until medium-firm (al dente) — about 3 minutes.
9) Carefully lift the strainer from the pot.
10) Pour noodles immediately into the ice bath to chill.
11) Repeat cooking and chilling until all noodles are done.
12) Transfer chilled noodles into blue strainers and let drain completely.
13) Place drained noodles into grey bins and drizzle lightly with oil to prevent sticking.
14) Weigh, label, lid, and store in the fridge until needed.

ingredients (per batch / 4" pan): Rice – 2 kg Lime leaves – 6–7 pieces Boiling water – 2.2 kg Instructions:
1) Preheat the red oven to 400°F.

3) Fill the steam kettle with water and bring it to a boil.
4) Divide the total dry rice by 2 kg/pan to determine how many hotel pans are required.
5) For each 4" hotel pan, add 2 kg rice, 6–7 lime leaves, and 2.2 kg boiling water. Mix gently with a spatula to ensure an even layer.
6) If the remaining rice for the final pan is over 1 kg, make another full pan. If the remaining rice is under 1 kg, split it evenly among the pans. Maximum per pan is 2.3 kg.
7) Wrap each pan tightly with aluminum foil and place in the oven. Bake for about 20 minutes, checking to ensure rice is cooked.
8) Remove pans from the oven. Wait until the rice is cool enough to handle. Remove lime leaves and fluff rice in the pan.
9) Gather scales, white scoops with grey bottoms, rinse cups, and baking sheets lined with parchment. Portion rice into balls weighing 135–140 g each. Place rice balls on parchment trays in a 5x7 layout.
10) Once trays are filled, wrap tightly with plastic wrap and place in the fridge.

Prep Instructions [012]:
1) Preheat the flat top if cooking to medium high heat.
2) Once hot, add oil to the cooking surface.
3) Add meat in batches of approximately 1.45 kg per rotation on the flat top. Avoid overcrowding to prevent boiling instead of searing.
4) Cook the meat for 5–10 minutes, chopping and turning it with a spatula to ensure even cooking.
5) Determine the total number of rotations needed for your batch. Divide the spice mixture accordingly.
6) When the meat is semi-cooked, add the first half of the divided spice mixture. Stir to coat evenly.
7) Continue cooking, then add the second half of the spice mixture during the final stage of cooking to ensure even flavor distribution.
8) Remove the meat from the flat top and place it onto trays to cool.
9) Once cooled, transfer the meat into bins, then weigh and label appropriately. Additional Notes for Large Batches: Pre-weigh trays or bins and divide the spice mixture in advance according to the number of rotations. Splitting the spice into two coatings per batch ensures even coverage and consistent flavor.

1) Set up 2 large rondeau pots on the rocket burners. You will need a canola oil bottle, and a metal paddle for stirring.
2) Pre-heat over medium heat for 2-3 mins (5-10 mins for the tilt skillet) with enough canola oil to coat the pot/skillet.
3) Once your oil in the pot/skillet is hot, add your onions. Stir regularly to prevent browning. Let them cook until translucent.
4) Add your beef, jalapeno, and garlic to the pot/skillet and cook while stirring regularly with a metal paddle to make sure nothing is sticking to the bottom.
5) When your beef is over 50% cooked, add your spices and salt and continue cooking until there is no pink/red colour left. Approximately 8-10 minutes, add more time when doing large batched if needed to achieve 100% cook on the meat.
6) When the beef is cooked fully, turn off the heat and remove from the pans into perforated deep hotel pans inside full deep hotel pans to allow the grease to drain from the meat if needed.
7) Once drained and slightly cooled, transfer the beef to clean full hotel pans and place on sheet trays on a rack and roll in the fridge to cool fully.
8) Once cooled, weigh your product, wrap with plastic, label with the name, date, weight and your initials.

1) Pre-heat the left oven on the large oven preset: Grilled Italian or for the small oven select the grill setting (see protein guide for photos), select level 5 darkness and set the probe temp to 165F (Ask the Protein Lead or Sous for a demo if needed).
2) Place the marinated thighs in a single layer on a parchment lined sheet tray.
3) Once the oven has finished pre-heating, place the trays of chicken into the oven. Select the largest piece by the edge and stick the probe into the thickest part of the breast -try to get as much of the probe into the chicken as possible as this will give the oven an optimal temp reading (Ask the Protein Lead or Sous for a demo if needed). Close the door to the oven.
4) Once the oven alarm goes off, the chicken is cooked. Open the door and remove the chicken. Place onto a rack and roll to cool slightly before placing the trays in the fridge.

6) Enter your weight into the Protein Prep Master List.

1) Pre-heat the Combi oven to 450F 0% steam Fan 5.
2) Gather some sheet trays and line them with parchment and give each one a light coating of cooking spray.
3) Using gloves, place the marinated salmon portions onto the trays with the skin side facing DOWN. Place Maxiumum 18 pieces per tray to allow enough space to get caramelization. (There is usually a brown or grey seam on this side, if you are un sure ask your Sous) We want to make sure the presentation side is facing up to get the caramelization desired. Note: if the tray is not full of portions, place a few metal serving spoons on the edges of the parchment to make sure the fan doesn't lift.
4) When the oven has pre-heated, place the trays inside and cook for 3 minutes. Remove from oven and pre-heat to 500F 0% Fan
5) Then flip the portions and brush them with the extra marinade (use fresh marinade not the raw contaminated one) and place back into the oven when its finished pre-heating. Cook for another 2 minutes.
6) When finished, place them into the fridge to fully cool.
7) Receive the portions by condensing them onto one tray with parchment and wrap them tightly (Be careful not to break or damage any of the portions).
8) Label each tray with the name, date, portion count and your initials.

1) Wash the carrots thoroughly. Trim off the tip and tail, then cut the carrots into 2-inch pieces to help them fit easily into the hopper and process evenly.
2) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E before setup. Open the continuous feed head and begin by placing the ejection disc onto the motor shaft inside the bowl. Next, install the 2x2mm julienne disc directly on the shaft. No scraper blade is needed for julienne cuts. Close the lid and make sure it is locked into place.
3) Plug in and turn on the Robot Coupe. Place the carrot pieces upright in the hopper (so the length of the carrot stands vertically), then guide them through using the pusher. This orientation helps produce long, clean julienne cuts.
4) Once all carrots are processed, collect them in a clean grey bin. Weigh, label with the item name and date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Take apart the celery from the base and separate the leaves from the stalks. Discard any damaged or wilted leaves.
2) Wash the celery thoroughly to ensure all dirt and debris are removed.
3) Set up the Robot Coupe by installing the ejection disc onto the motor shaft, followed by the 1x1 inch dicer grid. Install the 1x1 inch dicer blade above the grid and close the lid securely.
4) Process the celery by placing the stalks into the hopper. Use steady, consistent pressure to feed the celery through the dicer, ensuring they are evenly diced into 1x1 inch pieces.
5) Receive the diced celery into clean grey bins. Weigh, label with the item name and date, and place in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E and open the continuous feed head. Place the ejection disc onto the motor shaft, then insert the 1/1 inch (20mm) dicing grid into the bowl and attach the matching knife blade on the shaft above the grid. Close and lock the lid securely.

Sub-Recipe Name [006]: Cooked Pearl Tri Color Couscous (Sauce T)
Prep Instructions [012]:
1) Mise out all couscous.
2) Boil water in 40L pot like you would pasta, add 20g of oil and 20g of salt.
3) Divide your total dry amount by 5kg and thats how many rounds you will need to do- keep in mind 5 kg dry= about 15kg cooked
4) Add your round of couscous to the water and cook for about 6 mins, pull from heat and use strainer to scoop out the couscous and put it into a blue bin.
5) Then once almost done strain the last bit.
6) Put a black lid upside down underneath the blue strainer bin, drizzle with oil and toss before lidding once cooled.
7) Weigh and label.

1) In a bowl, weigh out the minced garlic and ginger, onions and canola oil.
2) In a separate bowl weigh all your spices other than salt (use a mini scale if spice weights are less than 20g for best accuracy). Keep salt separate.
3) Weigh the veg stock and coconut milk, tomatoes, veg stock, and peanut butter together. Save lemon juice for the end.
4) Heat a large pot or pre-heat the tilt skillet at medium. Add your canola and aromatics and heat them while stirring for 4 minutes, until the onions are translucent.
5) Add your spices and tomato paste, cook for 1 minute until fragrant.
6) Add the liquid ingredients and whisk to combine.
7) Bring the sauce to a simmer and cover while stirring every so often to prevent burning on the bottom. Cook for 10-15 minutes.
8) Turn off the heat and stir in the lemon juice and salt. Taste to check seasoning (ask the sous if you are unsure).
9) Receive into grey bins (do not fill them more than 2/3) and let cool using ice wands before placing in the fridge.
10) Remove the ice wands and weigh the sauce, label each bin with the name, weight, date, number of bins, and your initials.
11) Place the bins in the fridge and lid them if they are fully cooled.

Ingredient Name [008]: Canned Fire Roasted Diced Tomato(GFS Brand)

Prep Instructions [012]:
1) In a bowl, weigh out the minced garlic and ginger, onions and canola oil.
2) In a separate bowl weigh all your spices (use a mini scale for best accuracy).
3) Weigh the veg stock and coconut milk together and keep the lentils and lime juice in separate bowls.
4) Heat a large pot or pre-heat the tilt skillet at medium. Add your canola and aromatics and heat them while stirring for 3-4 minutes, until the onions are translucent.
5) Add your veg stock, coconut, oat milk and lentils, whisk to combine.
6) Bring the sauce to a simmer while stirring every so often to prevent burning on the bottom. Cook for 1 hour - 1hour and 30 minutes.
7) When the lentils are fully cooked the sauce should have thickened slightly.
8) Increase the heat and until it is simmering hard and add the cornstarch and water. Mix to combine until thickened. Turn off the heat and stir in the lime juice.
9) Receive into grey bins (do not fill them more than 2/3) and let cool using ice wands before placing in the fridge.
10) Remove the ice wands and weigh the sauce, label each bin with the name, weight, date, number of bins, and your initials.
11) Place the bins in the fridge and lid them if they are fully cooled.

1) Wash the beets thoroughly under cold water to remove any dirt. Trim off both the top (stem end) and bottom (root tip). If the beets are too large to fit into the hopper of the Robot Coupe, cut them in half or into quarters as needed.
2) Unplug the Robot Coupe CL52E before handling any blades. Open the continuous feed head by lifting the lid. Begin by placing the ejection disc onto the motor shaft — this disc spins to push product out of the chamber after it is cut. Next, insert the 4/5 dicing grid into the bowl, making sure it sits securely above the ejection disc. Finally, mount the matching 4/5 slicing disc on the shaft above the grid. This disc will slice the beets, and the slices will be immediately pushed through the grid to form uniform cubes. Close the lid and ensure it locks securely — the machine will not operate unless fully latched.
3) Plug in and turn on the Robot Coupe. Use the large feed chute to load the beets into the machine, and guide them through using the pusher. Apply steady, gentle pressure without forcing the beets through too quickly to ensure clean, even cuts.
4) Once all beets are diced, collect them in a clean container. Weigh, label, date.

Prep Instructions [012]:
1) Fill 60L pot 2/3 full with hot tap water. Add in each pot 100g of salt and 70g of oil to the pot (solid squirt from the oil bottle)
2) Place the pot on the rocket burner on max heat and bring to a boil. Cover with a lid to speed the process up.
3) Weigh out the dry pasta into a home depot bucket max weigh is 10kgs.
4) Add the pasta to the water once boiling (max 10kg per pot), and stir very frequently to prevent from sticking.
5) Boil pasta until 80% cooked, around 9 minutes to cook.
6) While the pasta is cooking, prepare two orange pails of cold tap water to later pour over noodles to flash cool them.
7) Make sure to clean and reserve the sinks for the next steps. In this next step, you will have to work fast to ensure the pasta doesnt over cook.
8) Once the pasta is ready to be drained, place the pot on the cart with the sheet tray underneath, ask a kitchen friend to help you lift to the sink, Slowly pour out the boiling water from the pot.
9) Once majority of the boiling water has been drained from the pot, pour the pasta into the strainer bin. And pour the cold water ontop to stop the cooking process.
10) Making sure to stir the pasta to eliminate the hot spots. Check the pasta to make sure it is properly cool, if not add more hot water. Once cool make sure all pasta water has drained out.
11) Once cool place the blue strainer, on top of a gray bin lid to catch any access water. Lightly drizzle oil on the pasta and to prevent the pasta from sticking. Make sure to stir the pasta with the oil to ensure all pasta is coated.
12) Weigh, label and place in the assigned area in the fridge.

Prep Instructions [012]:
1) Preheat the flat top if cooking to medium high heat.
2) Once hot, add oil to the cooking surface.
3) Add meat in batches of approximately 1.45 kg per rotation on the flat top. Avoid overcrowding to prevent boiling instead of searing.
4) Cook the meat for 5–10 minutes, chopping and turning it with a spatula to ensure even cooking.
5) Determine the total number of rotations needed for your batch. Divide the spice mixture accordingly.
6) When the meat is semi-cooked, add the first half of the divided spice mixture. Stir to coat evenly.
7) Continue cooking, then add the second half of the spice mixture during the final stage of cooking to ensure even flavor distribution.
8) Remove the meat from the flat top and place it onto trays to cool.
9) Once cooled, transfer the meat into bins, then weigh and label appropriately. Additional Notes for Large Batches: Pre-weigh trays or bins and divide the spice mixture in advance according to the number of rotations. Splitting the spice into two coatings per batch ensures even coverage and consistent flavor.

1) Preheat the Rational on Steam, maximum steam (100°C / 212°F saturated steam). Allow the oven to fully preheat before loading.
2) Make sure the prawn bags are fully thawed and well drained. Put on gloves.
3) Open two bags of shrimp/prawns and evenly distribute them into perforated hotel pans in a single layer. Do not overcrowd; prawns should not be piled.
4) Once the oven is preheated, load the perforated pans into the Rational with space between pans to allow proper steam circulation.
5) Steam on maximum steam for 5 minutes.
6) After 5 minutes, carefully remove the pans. Check doneness. The prawns are fully cooked when they are opaque white and pink with no blue, grey, or translucent areas. If unsure, ask your Station Lead or Sous to confirm.
7) Transfer the cooked prawns immediately onto a clean tray or solid hotel pan lined with parchment, spreading them out to stop carryover cooking.
8) Allow the prawns to cool slightly at room temperature, then place uncovered in the fridge until fully cooled.
9) Once fully cooled, portion the prawns into 70 g portions onto trays lined with parchment.
10) Wrap tightly with plastic, label with the date, your initials, the weight per portion, and include the total portion count on the label.
11) Place finished portions in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) In a large rondeau, add canola oil to coat the bottom, preheat it over the rocket burner at high heat.
2) Add maximum 1 bag of stew beef to the rondeau per batch (discard any liquid from the bag before adding). Sear it to get nice browned edges (do not fully cook!). If the bottom of the pot is getting dark, switch to a new one. Transfer each batch into its own deep hotel pan. Repeat until all the beef is browned.
3) Preheat the large combi oven to 284 F at 60% steam and level 3 fan.
4) Using gloves, break up the chorizo meat (into ground beef consistency), divide the total amount needed into each hotel pan of browned beef. Combine the onion and garlic together in a bowl, and divide between the hotel pans.
5) In a bucket, add all remaining ingredients (EXCEPT BAY LEAVES) and mix with a whisk to combine. Divide the amount between each hotel pan of beef and chorizo.
6) Mix the liquid with the meat in each pan. Divide the number of bay leaves between the pans, take out a scoop of stew, place the bay leaves in and cover with stew to ensure they remain whole.
7) Cover the hotel pan tightly with tinfoil.
8) When the oven has been preheated, place the hotel pans inside and cook for 1.5 hours.
9) After 1.5 hours pull out and remove tin foil, place back inside the oven for 1 more hour uncovered.
10) Remove a tray to check the doneness of the beef. It should be tender not chewy. Add another 30 minutes if it needs some more time when doing large batches.
11) When the beef is tender, remove from the oven and cool slightly before placing in the fridge. Receive the stew into grey bins (do not exceed 12kg per bin) Record the total weight.
12) Lid when cool, and label each bin with the name, weight, date, and your initials. HF updated procedure and base weight Dec 6, loss% = 67%

Prep Instructions [012]:
1) Fill a big pot with water and bring to a full boil.
2) Set a rice strainer inside a grey bin. Empty 10 packs of yam noodles into the rice strainer (maximum 3.5kg per bin).
3) Pour boiling water over the noodles until fully submerged and covered.
4) Leave uncovered and soak for 15–20 minutes until noodles are fully softened.
5) Check at 15 minutes for doneness.
6) While noodles are soaking, prepare two pails of cold tap water for flash cooling.
7) Once noodles are softened, lift the rice strainer and transfer noodles into a blue straining bin.
8) Immediately pour cold water over the noodles to flash cool.
9) Once fully chilled, transfer noodles back into a rice strainer and place into a grey storage bin with a grey lid underneath to catch excess water.
10) Store in the fridge until portioning day.

Pre-heat the left oven on the grill setting (see protein guide for photos), select level 5 darkness and set the probe temp to 160F (Ask the Protein Lead or Sous for a demo if needed). Using clean gloved hands, mix the chicken until all pieces are evenly coated with oil and seasoning. Place the seasoned chicken breasts onto trays lined with parchment (No more than 5x6 or 30 units per tray). Once the oven has finished pre-heating, place the trays of chicken into the oven. Select the largest piece by the edge and stick the probe into the thickest part of the breast — try to get as much of the probe into the chicken as possible as this will give the oven an optimal temp reading (Ask the Protein Lead or Sous for a demo if needed). Close the oven door. Once the oven alarm goes off, remove the cooked breasts and place onto a rack and roll to cool slightly before placing the trays in the fridge. Once cool, wrap with plastic (if you are not slicing them right away), label with the name, weight, date, and your initials.

Ingredient Name [008]: Defrost Chicken Breast For Client Units (salad Container)(Pro W)

Prep Instructions [012]:
1) Preheat the pan: Place a non-stick skillet over medium heat and let it warm 1–2 minutes.
2) Prepare the bacon: Lay the turkey bacon strips in a single layer. Avoid overlapping for even cooking.
3) Cook the first side: Let the strips cook 4–5 minutes, allowing the edges to brown slightly. 4 )Flip the bacon: Use tongs to turn each strip over. Cook another 4–5 minutes until lightly browned and mostly crispy.
4) Add the maple drizzle: Lower heat slightly. Drizzle with the maple syrup over the bacon strips in the pan. Let it bubble and caramelize for 30–60 seconds, spooning it over the strips to coat evenly.
5) Drain and serve: Transfer bacon into a perforated hotel pan and let cool.

1) Preheat oven to 400 °F.
2) Place sausages on a baking sheet lined with parchment and spray lightly with oil on the tray and ontop of the sausages.

4) Remove from heat and allow time to cool + label.

1) Pre-heat the tilt skillet on high for 5-10 mins. Add enough canola oil to coat the whole skillet.
2) Once the skillet is hot, add your meat balls - make sure not to add too many as they will not brown.
3) Sear all sides of each meatball until they are golden brown. Use tongs or spatulas to flip them.
4) When each piece is perfectly browned, turn off the skillet and remove from heat.
5) Place the meat balls into hotel pans and place them on sheet trays on a rack and roll in the fridge to cool.
6) Once they have cooled 100%, wrap the pans in plastic and label with the amount of pans (ex. 1/2, 2/2), date, number of units and your initials.
7) Enter your unit count in the Protein Prep Master List.

1) Mise the pre-washed mint.
2) Pluck leaves from any remaining thick stems, discarding only the large or woody ones—thin tender stems can be kept.
3) Gather the plucked mint into a tight pile on the cutting board.
4) Using a sharp chef’s knife, chop the mint with a smooth rocking motion, keeping the tip of the knife in contact with the board.
5) Re-gather the pile with your knife and rotate the board or pile to ensure an even chop.
6) Continue chopping until the mint is finely chopped—smaller than a rough chop, but not minced.
7) Avoid over-chopping to prevent bruising and excess moisture.
8) Transfer the chopped mint into a gray bin.
9) Weigh, label, and store in the fridge in the assigned area.

1) Set up a large rondeau pot on the rocket burner or pre-heat the tilt skillet (depending on quantity needed to cook) You will need a canola oil bottle, and a metal paddle for stirring.
2) Pre-heat over medium heat for 2-3 mins (5-10 mins for the tilt skillet) with enough canola oil to coat the pot/skillet.
3) Once your oil in the pot/skillet is hot, add the garlic, and yellow onion. Stir regularly to prevent browning. Let them cook until the onions are translucent and fragrant.
4) Add your ground meat to the pot/skillet and cook for 5-10 mins. Stir regularly with a metal paddle to make sure nothing is sticking to the bottom.

1) Remove any damaged outer leaves from the cabbage, then cut the head into quarters and remove the core from each section. If the quarters are still too large to fit in the hopper, cut them into smaller wedges.

6) Add your oil and veg, cook for 4-5 mins until onions become translucent.

8) Once your spices and veg are caramelized add your coconut sugar and liquids, mix to combine.
9) Bring the sauce to a simmer and let it cook for 12 mins. Add the garam and fenugreek and simmer for 3 mins.
10) Taste check the sauce for seasoning (ask your sous or station lead if you are unsure).
11) Remove from heat and cool.
12) Weigh the sauce, label bins with name, weight, date, and your initials.
